name: Philodendron lacerum
aboutText: Philodendron lacerum is a climbing aroid species native to the Caribbean islands, including Cuba, Hispaniola, and Jamaica. It grows as an epiphyte or hemiepiphyte, climbing strongly up tree trunks in moist, humid forest environments. The plant develops large, deeply incised, or lobed leaves that are glossy green with a leathery texture. The margins are wavy and heavily scalloped, giving it a distinctive, prehistoric appearance.

propagation.notes: Single-node cuttings root readily in moist sphagnum or water. Maintain 70%+ humidity and bottom warmth for best results.

careGuide.substrate: High-porosity mix: 50% perlite, 30% orchid bark, 20% coco coir. Good aeration is essential — philodendrons are prone to root rot in dense substrates.
careGuide.watering: Water thoroughly when the top 3–4 cm of substrate is dry. Ensure free drainage and never allow to sit in water. Reduce frequency in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 60–80% preferred. UK tap water can cause brown tips — use rainwater or filtered water where possible.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser (e.g. 20-20-20) at quarter strength every two weeks in spring and summer. Monthly at most in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months or when roots circle the base of the pot. Move up one pot size only — oversized pots increase rot risk.

commonProblems.cause: Low humidity or fluoride/chlorine in tap water
commonProblems.fix: Increase ambient humidity and switch to rainwater or filtered water
Allow substrate to dry further between waterings; check root health and repot if necessary
Move to a brighter position with more indirect light

items: Check that roots are firm and white to tan — avoid plants with black, slimy, or absent roots
Inspect leaf undersides for thrips (tiny dark specks or silvery streaking) and mealybugs (white cottony deposits at leaf axils)
Verify the plant has at least 2–3 healthy leaves and shows signs of active growth
For listings showing only one photo, request images of the roots, underside of leaves, and any new growth before purchasing

fieldNotes.title: Caribbean Island Endemism
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: A Philodendron from the Caribbean islands — Hispaniola, Cuba, and Jamaica — which immediately distinguishes it from the vast majority of collectable Philodendron that originate in mainland South and Central America. Lacerum means 'torn' or 'lacerated' — a reference to the deeply incised, irregular leaf margins that give mature specimens a somewhat ragged, dramatically divided silhouette. Island endemics carry an inherent scarcity value beyond what the trade price suggests: restricted populations in declining habitat. At middle tier pricing.
